content.assessment: Migrated lens reading for 'Non-structural compensatory reform (leave selection age unchanged)'. Selection reform is now live government policy with EU tailwind but faces low-probability cross-party durability, high reversal risk on government turnover, and non-negotiable stakeholder preconditions (workforce guarantees, statutory funding) that the minister has not yet addressed, making it politically feasible in the medium term (2027–2028) only if the government narrows its scope to pilot-phase rebalancing coupled with explicit quality-levelling commitments, abandons abolition rhetoric, and negotiates binding funding guarantees—the opposite of which suggests either stalled-indefinite consultation or legislative reversal within one electoral cycle.
opportunities: Achievement gains in the range of Portugal's observed 0.3 SD improvement over roughly 15-18 years are plausible for the comprehensive (non-tracked) majority of Hungarian pupils if curriculum, autonomy, and assessment reforms are implemented with comparable fidelity.

Politically less disruptive than structural reform since it does not touch the statutory definition of secondary education, avoiding the felmenő-rendszer obligations and the direct Fidesz 'totalitarian unified model' framing risk that S3 faces — though this should not be read as evidence the package is politically risk-free, given that targeted funding for disadvantaged schools is independently a fiscal-consolidation target.
strengths: The Tisza Party's 12 April 2026 electoral victory (141 of 199 seats, ~53% party-list) created a constitutional two-thirds mandate; education received a separate ministry for the first time since 2010, signalling structural reform is now institutionally feasible.

Education Minister Lannert Judit (June 2026) publicly stated that 6/8-year gimnázium students do not develop faster than age-cohort peers; the system's only function is elite self-selection and socioeconomic status reproduction, not pedagogy. She framed the question as a societal-policy choice, not technical, and explicitly committed to review rather than abolition, with current gimnáziums to retain their position.
threats: Targeted TEIP-style funding lines are likely first-line cuts during fiscal consolidation (deficit projected at 6.2% in 2026, debt rising to 76.8% of GDP by 2027), making the central benefit-delivery mechanism budget-vulnerable in a way that significantly undermines the durability of any expected equity gain, even though the scenario avoids the structural-reform reversal risks facing S1-S3.

The scenario's political attractiveness as a 'lower-risk' alternative may itself become a political liability if framed by reform advocates or the European Commission as inadequate given Hungary's documented status as the EU's most socially selective system, risking accusations of policy avoidance rather than genuine reform.
weaknesses: Will the government publish a formal legislative timetable, Oktatási Hivatal review mandate, or government resolution on selection reform, moving it beyond consultative intent?

Are statutory (vs annual-budget) funding-floor guarantees politically achievable under the projected fiscal consolidation pressures, or will compensatory funding remain contingent on annual budget cycles?
